supplementEntry.name: GlyNAC
supplementEntry.evidenceTierRationale: One well-designed placebo-controlled RCT (n=24 OA, Kumar/Sekhar 2023) plus one independent RCT by Nestle/Profil (n=114, Lizzo 2022). Supporting pilot and open-label trials. Nearly all clinical work from a single research group (Sekhar lab, Baylor). The independent RCT failed its primary endpoint in full cohort. Limits to Tier 2 pending large multi-center replication.

cognitive: Cognitive function improvement in aging populations
nonCognitive: Glutathione repletion and antioxidant defense restoration
Physical function improvement (gait speed, grip strength, 6-min walk)
Body composition improvement (reduced waist circumference, body fat)

supplementEntry.optimalDuration: 14 days to 24 weeks (benefits decline on withdrawal)
mechanismsOfAction: Glutathione synthesis restoration via rate-limiting precursors glycine and cysteine
Reactive oxygen species neutralization through enhanced GSH availability
Nutrient sensing pathway normalization (mTOR, AMPK, Sirtuin)
Genomic stability protection via reduced oxidative DNA damage

commonSideEffects: Mild GI discomfort at higher doses
contraindications: Active bleeding disorders (NAC has mild anticoagulant properties)

effectSizes.glutathioneRestoration: Corrected to levels comparable with young adults (RCT, n=24)
effectSizes.physicalFunction: Significant improvements in gait speed, grip strength, and 6-min walk vs placebo (RCT, n=24)

keyCitations.title: Supplementing Glycine and N-Acetylcysteine (GlyNAC) in Older Adults Improves Glutathione Deficiency, Oxidative Stress, Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Inflammation, Physical Function, and Aging Hallmarks: A Randomized Clinical Trial
keyCitations.journal: The Journals of Gerontology: Series A

keyCitations.title: Glycine and N-acetylcysteine (GlyNAC) supplementation in older adults improves glutathione deficiency, oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, inflammation, insulin resistance, endothelial dysfunction, genotoxicity, muscle strength, and cognition: Results of a pilot clinical trial

Supplementing Glycine and N-acetylcysteine (GlyNAC) in Aging HIV Patients Improves Oxidative Stress, Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Inflammation, Endothelial Dysfunction, Insulin Resistance, Genotoxicity, Strength, and Cognition
GlyNAC Supplementation Improves Impaired Mitochondrial Fuel Oxidation and Lowers Insulin Resistance in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes
A Randomized Controlled Clinical Trial in Healthy Older Adults to Determine Efficacy of Glycine and N-Acetylcysteine Supplementation on Glutathione Redox Status and Oxidative Damage

GlyNAC Supplementation Improves Glutathione Deficiency, Oxidative Stress, Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Inflammation, Aging Hallmarks, Metabolic Defects, Muscle Strength, Cognitive Decline, and Body Composition: Implications for Healthy Aging

mechanisms.mechanism: Glutathione synthesis restoration via glycine and cysteine precursor supply

evidence.title: Supplementing Glycine and N-Acetylcysteine (GlyNAC) in Older Adults Improves Glutathione Deficiency, Oxidative Stress, Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Inflammation, Physical Function, and Aging Hallmarks: A Randomized Clinical Trial
evidence.journal: The Journals of Gerontology: Series A
evidence.sampleSize: n=24 older adults (12 GlyNAC vs 12 placebo) + 12 young adults
evidence.findings: 16 weeks of GlyNAC corrected GSH deficiency to levels comparable with young adults, reduced oxidative stress, improved mitochondrial function, and corrected multiple aging hallmarks vs placebo.

evidence.title: GlyNAC Supplementation Improves Impaired Mitochondrial Fuel Oxidation and Lowers Insulin Resistance in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes
evidence.findings: 14 days GlyNAC improved mitochondrial fatty-acid oxidation by 30% (p<0.001), lowered mitochondrial glucose oxidation by 47% (p<0.01), decreased insulin resistance by 22% (p<0.01), lowered plasma FFA by 25% (p<0.01).
evidence.methodology: Open-label pilot study with non-diabetic controls

evidence.title: Glycine and N-acetylcysteine (GlyNAC) supplementation in older adults improves glutathione deficiency, oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, inflammation, insulin resistance, endothelial dysfunction, genotoxicity, muscle strength, and cognition: Results of a pilot clinical trial
evidence.findings: 24 weeks GlyNAC improved inflammation markers, reduced oxidative stress, corrected mitochondrial dysfunction. Benefits declined after 12 weeks of stopping supplementation.
evidence.methodology: Open-label pilot trial with young adult comparators

mechanisms.mechanism: Nutrient sensing pathway normalization (mTOR, AMPK, Sirtuin)

evidence.title: GlyNAC Supplementation in Mice Increases Length of Life by Correcting Glutathione Deficiency, Oxidative Stress, Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Abnormalities in Mitophagy and Nutrient Sensing, and Genomic Damage
evidence.findings: GlyNAC-supplemented mice lived 24% longer than controls. Improved GSH synthesis, oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, mitophagy, nutrient sensing (mTOR, AMPK, Sirtuin pathways), and genomic damage in heart, liver, and kidney.
evidence.methodology: Controlled mouse lifespan study with supplementation vs control diet

benefits.specificClaim: Corrects multiple aging hallmarks and improves physical function in older adults
benefits.replicationStatus: Replicated across pilot and RCT by same group; independent replication limited

evidence.title: Supplementing Glycine and N-Acetylcysteine (GlyNAC) in Older Adults Improves Glutathione Deficiency, Oxidative Stress, Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Inflammation, Physical Function, and Aging Hallmarks: A Randomized Clinical Trial
evidence.journal: The Journals of Gerontology: Series A
evidence.findings: 16 weeks GlyNAC corrected GSH deficiency, oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, insulin resistance, aging hallmarks. Improved gait speed, muscle strength, 6-minute walk, body composition, and systolic blood pressure vs placebo.

evidence.title: Glycine and N-acetylcysteine (GlyNAC) supplementation in older adults improves glutathione deficiency, oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, inflammation, insulin resistance, endothelial dysfunction, genotoxicity, muscle strength, and cognition: Results of a pilot clinical trial
evidence.findings: 24 weeks GlyNAC corrected GSH deficiency, oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction; improved inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, insulin resistance, genomic damage, cognition, strength, gait speed, exercise capacity, lowered body fat and waist circumference. Benefits declined after 12 weeks of stopping.
evidence.methodology: Open-label pilot trial with young adult comparators

benefits.specificClaim: Improves mitochondrial fuel oxidation and reduces insulin resistance in type 2 diabetes
benefits.replicationStatus: Single pilot study, not yet replicated

evidence.title: GlyNAC Supplementation Improves Impaired Mitochondrial Fuel Oxidation and Lowers Insulin Resistance in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes
evidence.findings: 30% improvement in mitochondrial fatty-acid oxidation (p<0.001), 22% decrease in insulin resistance (p<0.01), 25% reduction in plasma FFA (p<0.01).
evidence.methodology: Open-label pilot study with non-diabetic controls

benefits.specificClaim: Improves cognition in older adults and HIV patients with premature aging
benefits.replicationStatus: Observed in pilot and HIV trials; no dedicated cognitive RCT

evidence.title: Supplementing Glycine and N-acetylcysteine (GlyNAC) in Aging HIV Patients Improves Oxidative Stress, Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Inflammation, Endothelial Dysfunction, Insulin Resistance, Genotoxicity, Strength, and Cognition
evidence.findings: 12 weeks GlyNAC improved cognition, physical function, GSH, mitochondrial function, oxidative stress, inflammation, endothelial function, genomic damage, insulin resistance in PWH. Benefits receded 8 weeks after stopping.
evidence.methodology: Open-label trial with matched non-HIV controls

evidence.title: GlyNAC Supplementation in Old Mice Improves Brain Glutathione Deficiency, Oxidative Stress, Glucose Uptake, Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Genomic Damage, Inflammation and Neurotrophic Factors to Reverse Age-Associated Cognitive Decline
evidence.findings: 8 weeks GlyNAC in old mice corrected brain GSH deficiency, oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, inflammation, genomic damage, improved neurotrophic factors and reversed age-associated cognitive decline.
evidence.methodology: Controlled mouse study comparing old supplemented, old unsupplemented, and young mice

benefits.specificClaim: Improves endothelial function and blood pressure in older adults
benefits.replicationStatus: Observed as secondary outcome in RCT and pilot

evidence.title: Supplementing Glycine and N-Acetylcysteine (GlyNAC) in Older Adults Improves Glutathione Deficiency, Oxidative Stress, Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Inflammation, Physical Function, and Aging Hallmarks: A Randomized Clinical Trial
evidence.journal: The Journals of Gerontology: Series A
evidence.findings: GlyNAC corrected endothelial dysfunction and improved systolic blood pressure vs placebo in older adults.

safety.claim: Safe and well-tolerated across multiple clinical trials up to 24 weeks

evidence.title: Supplementing Glycine and N-Acetylcysteine (GlyNAC) in Older Adults Improves Glutathione Deficiency, Oxidative Stress, Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Inflammation, Physical Function, and Aging Hallmarks: A Randomized Clinical Trial
evidence.journal: The Journals of Gerontology: Series A
evidence.findings: GlyNAC was safe and well-tolerated at full dose (~14g/day) for 16 weeks in older adults. No serious adverse events reported.

evidence.title: A Randomized Controlled Clinical Trial in Healthy Older Adults to Determine Efficacy of Glycine and N-Acetylcysteine Supplementation on Glutathione Redox Status and Oxidative Damage
evidence.findings: GlyNAC at 3 doses (2.4g, 4.8g, 7.2g/day) for 2 weeks was safe and well tolerated in 114 healthy older adults. No serious adverse events.
evidence.methodology: Independent industry RCT (Nestle/Profil) with dose-response design (NCT05041179)

safety.claim: Potential sex-dependent effects observed in preclinical cardiac aging model

evidence.title: Sex Differences in Response to Diet Enriched With Glutathione Precursors in the Aging Heart
evidence.journal: The Journals of Gerontology: Series A
evidence.findings: GlyNAC improved diastolic function and mitochondrial markers in old male mice but not females. GlyNAC-fed females showed a decline in exercise performance. Supports sex-specific responses requiring human confirmation.
evidence.methodology: Controlled mouse study with 12 weeks GlyNAC diet, examining sex differences

dosage.dosageRange: ~14g/day total (7g glycine + 7g NAC) based on body weight (~100 mg/kg each)
dosage.claim: Weight-based dosing used in pivotal Sekhar trials

evidence.title: Supplementing Glycine and N-Acetylcysteine (GlyNAC) in Older Adults Improves Glutathione Deficiency, Oxidative Stress, Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Inflammation, Physical Function, and Aging Hallmarks: A Randomized Clinical Trial
evidence.journal: The Journals of Gerontology: Series A
evidence.findings: Glycine 1.33 mmol/kg/day + NAC 0.81 mmol/kg/day (approximately 100 mg/kg each, ~7g+7g for 70kg adult) for 16 weeks was effective and well-tolerated.

dosage.claim: Lower fixed doses tested in independent trial with mixed efficacy results

evidence.title: A Randomized Controlled Clinical Trial in Healthy Older Adults to Determine Efficacy of Glycine and N-Acetylcysteine Supplementation on Glutathione Redox Status and Oxidative Damage
evidence.findings: Primary endpoint (total GSH increase) not met in full cohort. Post-hoc: subjects with high oxidative stress AND low baseline GSH who received medium/high doses showed significant GSH increase (p=0.016).
evidence.methodology: Independent industry RCT with dose-response design (NCT05041179)
